refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and that the acceptance criterion is customer-negotiated, making it a measurable, testable functional requirement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with the customer. Measure packet loss across the telemetry uplink (sensor→operator), tasking downlink (operator→vehicle), and SafetyAlert channels. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target for ≥95% of test duration.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The Teleoperation module shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The Teleoperation module shall implement adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) mechanisms to minimize packet loss under variable network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The Safety Services module shall monitor teleoperation packet loss and trigger SafetyAlert if loss exceeds threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The Vehicle module shall maintain command responsiveness even under transient packet loss events.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce effective packet loss.
DP-B: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and packet retransmission logic in the Teleoperation module to recover lost packets within latency budget.
DP-C: Implement network quality monitoring and adaptive bitrate/codec selection to reduce congestion-induced packet loss.
DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining FEC, adaptive QoS, and real-time packet loss monitoring with safety-critical alerting.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining forward error correction (FEC) for critical telemetry/tasking frames, adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) mechanisms (bitrate/codec adaptation, priority queuing) in the Teleoperation module, real-time packet loss monitoring with threshold-based SafetyAlert triggering in Safety Services, and graceful degradation logic in the Vehicle module to maintain safe operation under transient loss.

potential_sources: DP-D couples Teleoperation (FEC, QoS adaptation) with Safety Services (monitoring, alerting) and Vehicle (graceful degradation). This is acceptable because: (1) the coupling is intentional and safety-critical; (2) interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are well-defined; (3) each module has clear responsibility (Teleoperation owns QoS logic, Safety Services owns threshold monitoring, Vehicle owns safe fallback).
coupling_check.notes: The hybrid approach avoids over-reliance on any single mechanism. FEC handles random loss; QoS adaptation handles congestion; monitoring + alerting provides safety oversight. Decoupling risk is low because module boundaries align with functional responsibilities.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable under agreed network conditions with the selected mechanisms. Risk: If threshold is unrealistic (e.g., <0.1% on a congested 4G network), DP-D may be insufficient; mitigation: validate threshold feasibility during requirements negotiation.

Assumption: FEC overhead (redundant packets) does not exceed available bandwidth. Risk: On bandwidth-constrained networks, FEC may paradoxically increase loss; mitigation: implement adaptive FEC that scales with available bandwidth.

Assumption: Safety Services can monitor packet loss in real-time without introducing additional latency. Risk: Monitoring overhead could degrade performance; mitigation: use asynchronous, non-blocking monitoring.

Assumption: Vehicle module can safely degrade operation (e.g., reduced speed, autonomous hold) if packet loss exceeds threshold. Risk: Operator may not expect degradation; mitigation: provide clear SafetyAlert feedback and operator training.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget; (2) QoS adaptation triggers (e.g., packet loss >0.5% → reduce bitrate); (3) SafetyAlert latency requirement (e.g., <100ms); (4) Vehicle graceful degradation modes (e.g., speed limit, autonomous hold, operator override). These details will inform IM assignment and lower-level decomposition.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including: (1) packet loss detection and FEC encoding/decoding; (2) adaptive QoS mechanisms (bitrate/codec selection, priority queuing); (3) real-time telemetry/tasking frame transmission; (4) interface to Safety Services for loss monitoring; (5) interface to Vehicle for command delivery and feedback reception. The module abstracts network variability from the operator and vehicle control logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor data from Vehicle (camera, LIDAR, IMU, position, load, etc.). Teleoperation applies FEC encoding and adaptive compression before transmission to operator. Packet loss on this channel degrades operator situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound operator commands to Vehicle (movement, tool control, etc.). Teleoperation applies FEC encoding and priority queuing. Packet loss on this channel delays or loses commands, impacting safety and productivity.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation reports real-time packet loss metrics and threshold violations to Safety Services. SafetyAlert triggers operator notifications and vehicle graceful degradation if loss exceeds customer-defined limit.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network connection (4G, 5G, WiFi, satellite, or hybrid) between operator and vehicle. Teleoperation monitors Network Link quality (latency, jitter, available bandwidth) to inform QoS adaptation decisions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural home for packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, QoS adaptation, and real-time monitoring are core teleoperation responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface leaks to Safety Services (intentional, safety-critical coupling). High containment.

Safety Services could own packet loss monitoring and alerting, but would require deep knowledge of Teleoperation internals (FEC state, QoS metrics). This creates tight coupling and diffusion. Better to keep monitoring in Teleoperation and have Safety Services react to SafetyAlert events. Low containment.

Vehicle could implement graceful degradation (e.g., speed limiting on packet loss), but packet loss is a network phenomenon, not a vehicle phenomenon. Placing FEC/QoS logic in Vehicle would couple vehicle control to network details. Better to have Teleoperation manage network quality and Vehicle respond to Tasking delays. Low containment.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Packet loss mitigation is a subsystem concern, not a system-level concern. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all modules. Low containment.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (hybrid packet loss mitigation). Rationale: (1) Teleoperation already owns the communication pipeline and has visibility into packet loss; (2) FEC and QoS adaptation are communication-layer concerns, not vehicle or safety concerns; (3) Teleoperation can implement these mechanisms transparently without coupling to vehicle control logic; (4) SafetyAlert interface provides a clean, intentional coupling to Safety Services for safety-critical monitoring. Containment score of 95 reflects high alignment.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it owns FEC, QoS adaptation, monitoring, and alerting. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (e.g., FEC codec, QoS controller, packet loss monitor) at the next level.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface creates a dependency on Safety Services. If Safety Services is slow to respond, vehicle safety could be compromised. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert latency SLA (e.g., <100ms) and implement asynchronous, non-blocking alerting.

Risk: Network Link interface assumes the network is observable and controllable. If network is opaque (e.g., third-party carrier), QoS adaptation may be limited. Mitigation: design QoS adaptation to work with limited network visibility (e.g., infer congestion from packet loss patterns).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has sufficient computational and memory resources to implement FEC, QoS adaptation, and real-time monitoring without introducing unacceptable latency. If Teleoperation is resource-constrained, consider offloading FEC to a dedicated hardware accelerator (e.g., FPGA) or splitting QoS logic into a separate 'Network Quality Controller' sub-module. Also, clarify the ownership of Network Link: is it owned by Teleoperation, or is it a shared infrastructure module? If shared, define the interface contract (e.g., available bandwidth, latency estimates) that Teleoperation can rely on.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid FEC + QoS adaptation + monitoring + alerting) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). The DP is comprehensive, covering detection, mitigation, and safety response. No independence axiom violation: the DP is a single, coherent solution concept. Verification is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a one-way event stream (Teleoperation → Safety Services) to minimize coupling.

Network Link interface couples Teleoperation to network infrastructure. Mitigation: abstract network quality as a set of observable metrics (latency, jitter, loss) that Teleoperation can query without knowing network implementation details.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment for DP-D. FEC, QoS adaptation, and monitoring are all communication-layer concerns that naturally belong in Teleoperation. The only external coupling is SafetyAlert (intentional, safety-critical) and Network Link (necessary for QoS adaptation). Diffusion is minimal and justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via: (1) lab testing with simulated network conditions (packet loss injection, latency, jitter); (2) field testing under agreed customer network conditions; (3) continuous monitoring of packet loss metrics in production. Acceptance criterion (≤1% or customer-defined) is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns packet loss mitigation (FEC, QoS, monitoring); Safety Services owns threshold-based alerting and vehicle safety response; Vehicle owns graceful degradation. Each module has a well-defined responsibility. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are explicit and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify FEC scheme, QoS adaptation triggers, SafetyAlert latency SLA, and Vehicle graceful degradation modes. These details will inform lower-level decomposition and implementation.

Define the ownership and interface contract for Network Link. Is it owned by Teleoperation, or is it a shared infrastructure module? Clarify what network quality metrics Teleoperation can observe and control.
Establish a safety-critical requirement for SafetyAlert latency (e.g., <100ms) to ensure timely operator notification and vehicle response.

Conduct a feasibility study to confirm that the customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable with DP-D under the agreed network conditions. If not, negotiate a revised threshold or enhanced DP.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is non-atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (packet loss detection, QoS adaptation, safety monitoring, graceful degradation) that must be decomposed into lower-level FRs, DPs, and IMs. DP-D is sound and well-justified, but its implementation requires detailed design of FEC schemes, QoS algorithms, monitoring logic, and vehicle response strategies. The Teleoperation module assignment is solid (95% containment), but the module itself may need internal decomposition (e.g., FEC codec, QoS controller, packet loss monitor sub-modules) to manage complexity. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to detail the implementation.
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified no blocking issues (FR-DP gate: Pass; DP-IM gate: Pass; FR-IM gate: Pass). However, the recommended revisions indicate that DP-D requires refinement before implementation. Specifically: (1) FEC scheme selection (Reed-Solomon vs. Turbo vs. LDPC) impacts performance and resource consumption; (2) QoS adaptation triggers must be tuned to the customer's network conditions; (3) SafetyAlert latency SLA must be established; (4) Vehicle graceful degradation modes must be designed. These refinements are best addressed through lower-level decomposition, not revision at the current level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The Teleoperation module shall detect packet loss in real-time by monitoring frame acknowledgments and timeout events, and report loss metrics (percentage, rate, duration) to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

FR-NGHE-009.2: The Teleoperation module shall implement forward error correction (FEC) encoding on critical telemetry and tasking frames, with adaptive redundancy (0-50% overhead) based on observed packet loss rate and available bandwidth.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The Teleoperation module shall implement adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) mechanisms including priority queuing (critical frames first), bitrate adaptation (reduce resolution/frequency if loss >0.5%), and codec selection (H.264 vs. H.265 vs. MJPEG) based on network conditions.

FR-NGHE-009.4: The Safety Services module shall monitor packet loss metrics from Teleoperation via SafetyAlert interface and trigger operator notifications and vehicle graceful degradation if loss exceeds customer-defined threshold for >5 seconds.

FR-NGHE-009.5: The Vehicle module shall implement graceful degradation modes (speed limiting, autonomous hold, operator override) in response to Tasking delays or SafetyAlert events, maintaining safe operation under transient packet loss.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable under agreed network conditions with hybrid FEC + QoS adaptation approach.

Teleoperation module has sufficient computational resources (CPU, memory, bandwidth) to implement FEC, QoS adaptation, and real-time monitoring without introducing unacceptable latency (assumed <100ms for SafetyAlert).
Network Link is observable (latency, jitter, available bandwidth can be measured) and partially controllable (bitrate, codec can be adapted).
Vehicle module can safely degrade operation (speed limiting, autonomous hold) in response to packet loss without operator intervention.
Safety Services can respond to SafetyAlert events within <100ms to trigger operator notification and vehicle response.
Operator is trained to recognize and respond to SafetyAlert notifications (e.g., reduced video quality, speed limiting) and can take manual control if needed.
open_risks: Risk: FEC overhead may paradoxically increase packet loss on bandwidth-constrained networks. Mitigation: implement adaptive FEC that scales with available bandwidth; conduct lab testing with various network conditions.

Risk: QoS adaptation (bitrate reduction, codec switching) may degrade operator situational awareness. Mitigation: prioritize critical telemetry (position, obstacles) over non-critical (high-res video); provide operator control over QoS trade-offs.

Risk: SafetyAlert latency may exceed <100ms SLA if Safety Services is overloaded. Mitigation: implement asynchronous, non-blocking alerting; use dedicated safety-critical communication channel if available.

Risk: Vehicle graceful degradation may be unexpected by operator, causing confusion or unsafe behavior. Mitigation: provide clear SafetyAlert feedback (e.g., 'Packet loss detected, speed limited to 5 km/h'); implement operator override with explicit confirmation.

Risk: Customer-defined packet loss threshold may be unrealistic (e.g., <0.1% on congested 4G network). Mitigation: conduct feasibility study during requirements negotiation; propose tiered thresholds (e.g., 1% for normal operation, 5% for degraded operation).
evidence_needed: Lab test results: packet loss measurement with FEC + QoS adaptation under simulated network conditions (latency 50-500ms, jitter 10-100ms, loss 0-10%).
Field test results: packet loss measurement under agreed customer network conditions (e.g., 4G coverage area, WiFi hotspot, satellite link).
Feasibility study: confirmation that customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable with DP-D; if not, proposed revised threshold or enhanced DP.
Resource analysis: confirmation that Teleoperation module has sufficient CPU, memory, and bandwidth to implement FEC, QoS adaptation, and monitoring without exceeding latency budget.
Safety analysis: FMEA or FTA for packet loss scenarios; confirmation that Vehicle graceful degradation modes are safe and operator-acceptable.
Interface specification: detailed SafetyAlert event schema, Network Link quality metrics, Telemetry/Tasking frame formats with FEC encoding.
Operator training plan: documentation of SafetyAlert notifications, QoS degradation modes, and operator response procedures.
